<!-- =========================================================================
     NATIVE QUARTO CONSTRUCTS
     These are built into Quarto (not part of our custom styling layer). Shown
     here for completeness — a popular subset, not the full set.
     ========================================================================= -->

## Callout blocks

Quarto's five built-in callouts render as colored boxes in **both** HTML and PDF.
Use `::: {.callout-note}` (or `-tip`, `-warning`, `-important`, `-caution`); the
first `##` line becomes the title, or set `title="…"`.

::: {.callout-note}
## A note

General information the reader should notice.
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## A tip

Helpful advice or a shortcut.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## A warning

Something to be careful about.
:::

::: {.callout-important}
## Something important

Don't miss this.
:::

Callouts also take `appearance="simple"` (no colored header fill) and
`icon=false`, and can be made collapsible on the web with `collapse="true"`:

::: {.callout-caution collapse="true" appearance="simple"}
## Click to expand (collapsible, simple appearance)

Collapsible callouts fold on the web; in the PDF they render fully expanded.
:::

## Tabsets

`::: {.panel-tabset}` turns second-level headings into tabs on the web --- great for
showing the same thing several ways (languages, OSes, before/after).
In the PDF the
tabs become consecutive subsections.
Rendering the PDF prints a harmless warning:
`Warning: couldn't recognize format, using default tabset rendering`.
Quarto only ships tab layouts for a few formats (HTML, revealjs, ...),
so for Typst it falls back to plain consecutive sections.

::: {.panel-tabset}
### Python

```python
print("hello from Python")
```

### R

```r
print("hello from R")
```
:::

## Margin content

In the `article` layout you can push notes, figures, or citations into the right
margin with `::: {.column-margin}`.[^marginnote]

[^marginnote]: Footnotes like this one are also rendered in the margin in the
    article layout.

::: {.column-margin}
**Margin note.** This block sits in the margin next to the body text (HTML).
In the
PDF it falls back to a normal block.
:::

Body text continues here; the margin note above sits alongside it on a wide screen.

## Citations & bibliography

References work in **both** formats from a single BibTeX file.
Add entries to
`references.bib` (each with a citation key like `example2025`), cite them in the
text with `@`, and Quarto builds the reference list for you.

**Citing in text.** Wrap keys in square brackets for a parenthetical citation, or
use a bare `@key` to weave the author into the sentence:

  | Syntax                         | Renders as (IEEE)                                   |
  | :----------------------------- | :-------------------------------------------------- |
  | `[@example2025]`               | a bracketed numeric reference [@example2025]        |
  | `@example2025`                 | an in-text reference @example2025                   |
  | `[@example2025, p. 5]`         | with a locator [@example2025, p. 5]                 |
  | `[see @example2025; @web2024]` | prefix + multiple keys [see @example2025; @web2024] |

  : Citation syntax and how it renders.
    {#tbl-citations tbl-colwidths="[45, 55]"}

**Adding a reference.** Append a BibTeX record to `references.bib` --- `@article`,
`@book`, `@misc`, `@inproceedings`, etc. --- then cite its key.
Only cited entries
appear in the reference list unless you add `nocite`.
The two demo entries above
(`example2025`, `web2024`) live in that file.

**The reference list.** It renders wherever you place an empty `::: {#refs} :::`
div --- in this template that's under `# References` at the bottom of `index.qmd`.
Remove that div and Quarto appends the list at the end of the document instead.

**Choosing a citation style.** The style is set by a [CSL](https://citationstyles.org)
file via the `csl:` key.
This template ships **IEEE** (numeric).
Swap it for any
style from the [CSL repository](https://github.com/citation-style-language/styles) ---
e.g. `csl: apa.csl` (download it locally) for author--date.

::: {.callout-important}
## CSL is HTML-only in this template

`csl:` is set **under the `html:` format**, not at the document level.
Typst cannot
open a remote CSL URL and would error, so the **PDF falls back to Typst's built-in
numeric bibliography**.
If you switch the HTML to an author--date style, the PDF
won't match --- for a non-numeric PDF, set Typst's `bibliographystyle` instead (see
`index.qmd` front matter and the Quarto Typst docs).
:::
